data.title: Nvidia Stock Is Trading As If It's 2019 Again
data.description:
Introduction

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) managed to go up by a factor of >20 from April 2020 to mid-January 2025 before the recent correction and given its stably high valuation multiples amid the market's rising concerns about DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) and the US-China trade war, it was a question of time when we see a cash-out of "the peak-catchers". From December 31st to April 4th, the stock fell by ~30% (now following the recent relief rally, NVDA is down -17% on a YTD basis), and that correction was triggered by the general weakness of the market (and the tech niche in particular).

My Thesis Update

I first wrote about NVDA back in late February 2024 with a "Hold" rating, mainly discussing the investment cycles and their impacts on cyclical semiconductor stocks. After some upgrades and downgrades, following the actual data from the firm and the industry, my most recent rating remained bullish. In late January 2025, I argued that the DeepSeek launch was actually a tailwind for Nvidia, not an operational risk, and since that update, the NVDA stock is down just 6.32%, beating the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) index on a relative basis:

Given that the firm managed to release its fiscal Q4 FY2025 in late February and is still undergoing a correction, I think I should look at my rating again and assess whether NVDA deserves a downgrade this time.

So let's soberly take a look at the fundamentals, not follow the fear's narratives. I don't see a clear hit to the company's market position in the next few years, as the demand will likely keep expanding tremendously even from today's relatively higher base. What's also important - the stock has finally become a real bargain at its current price when we consider the forwarding estimates and apply a premium to them based on the historical trends. This is why I'm reiterating my "Buy" today.

My Updated Reasoning

No matter what fears try to tell us right now, the facts are here - the demand environment stays strong with Nvidia reporting full-year revenue of $130.5 billion (10-K released in late February), which is a 114% YoY increase. The Q4 top-line hit ~$35.6 billion (up 93% YoY), which is showing some deceleration compared to the previous quarters' growth rates, but the firm's transition to the Blackwell architecture and the expanding scope of AI applications seem to be just getting started. I mean, delivering ~$11 billion in Blackwell revenue in its first quarter, exceeding the management's internal expectations quite meaningfully, and it suggests an intense customer demand and Nvidia's supply chain execution, even amidst some obstacles. At the same time, NVDA's customers "are finishing builds or need immediate compute while awaiting Blackwell", as the CFO noted during the recent Morgan Stanley conference, so the existing Hopper H200 platform continues to sell well. From that, I see limited sales cannibalization from Blackwell - if that were the case, the Hopper H200 platforms wouldn't continue to see sequential growth in Q4 FY25 (everyone would be just waiting for the newest offering). As some data center build-outs were designed around the Hopper architecture, it's going to feel continued demand - that's why I think once Blackwell production fully ramps up and its supply becomes more widely available, we're unlikely to see a sharper deterioration in Nvidia's consolidated top-line growth rate from here on.

Given all that, the Wall Street analysts' forecasts regarding Nvidia's revenues seem a bit pessimistic when we consider post-2026 estimates: From FY2026 to FY2030, analysts see an expansion in sales by just 52%, while the Generative AI market's size is projected to increase by a factor of ~2.6 over the same time (based on Grand View Research's study).

That's why I'd bet on at least a few years of positive revenue surprises, akin to the 2020-2025 period:

Now turning my sight to the bottom-line expectations, I see that from FY2026 to FY2030, the market expects a cumulative expansion in Nvidia's EPS of ~55%, so there's going to be a minimal operating leverage and margin expansion (the market probably assumes that the current margins are already at peak).

Indeed, visually Nvidia's margins do look like they're peaking:

The fears of peaking margins are real because Blackwell GPUs are way more complex than Hopper (208 billion vs. 80 billion transistors), use a custom, likely more expensive TSMC 4NP process initially, and incorporate advanced packaging, especially the GB200 integrating a CPU and two GPUs. So cost-wise, they're going to carry higher COGS for the firm, and hence that might bring challenges in transferring those costs to the end buyers. We already saw Blackwell's impact on the margins in Q4 when Nvidia's GP margins dipped into the low 70s% range during "the initial, expedited Blackwell ramp". The company will have a wider pricing range depending on specs, but the basic B100 is priced similarly to the H100 ($30-35k vs $30-40k) despite offering substantially better performance - that's why I wouldn't cancel off the market's concern entirely. Anyway, I think the gradual shift in the sales mix towards higher-priced GB200 Superchips (at $60-70k) will be a game changer for Nvidia. If Blackwell delivers, say, 3-5x the performance or efficiency for ~2x the price, the value proposition will remain compelling and support the margins at least where they are now.

Also, the Q4 dip in margin was due to the initial ramp, so assumingly, once production scales, manufacturing yields improve, the TSMC 4NP process matures, and Nvidia gains economies of scale, the per-unit COGS for Blackwell should decrease, and so one of the marginality pressures would vanish in the medium term.

So given all that, if we assume that today's marginality is indeed at a peak, but this peak doesn't necessarily mean that the margin contraction is coming, then in this case the EBITDA generation (and hence the flow-through to FCF) will likely stay in place.

With the same level of EBITDA transferring to the FCF, Nvidia's cash capacity for increasing buybacks will be there. In FY2025, the firm spent ~$40.6 billion on share repurchases, which, according to Seeking Alpha Premium, is about 2.3 times more than during FY2024. With margins staying where they are now (or even a bit lower), the firm's FCF would be enough to buy back more and hence reduce the denominator for the forwarding EPS more meaningfully compared to what seems to be priced in right now.

It's impossible to ignore the geopolitical concerns, and the US-China trade war in particular right now. The US export controls (before the implementation of all the tariffs) have already lowered sales to China - the CFO Colette Kress noted that "revenue percentage from the region is roughly half of what it was before the controls". Also, the recently announced "tariff exemptions" on smartphones, electronics, and chips - what helped the NVDA and the whole market close the previous week in green - "will be short-lived", The Guardian cited Trump in its just-published note:

So with PCBs, memory modules, and different chip components assembled in China (and also South Asia), the firm's COGS might stay under pressure, for sure. But at the same time, as we're talking about the geopolitics here, I should note that "sovereign AI initiatives" are taking hold as well, with countries like India and Japan making massive investments in Nvidia-powered AI factories to build national capabilities. As the world's globalization is gradually replaced by deglobalization with major countries trying to secure their national independence (in tech, AI, supply chain, etc.), I believe Nvidia's offerings will stay in high demand, and that demand won't be as elastic as most consumer goods (because the consumers in this case are governments with much more resources).

So far, the fundamentals of Nvidia's business seem to be exceptional, and even if we look at NVDA's stock price chart, we see a clear high-probability setup for a great reversal to the upside:

At least this is how I interpret the chart.

When it comes to the stock's valuation, I think now it looks way more tempting than at the end of calendar 2024. On a forwarding basis, the stock is valued at just 24.5x earnings, which is the lowest point in the past 6 years. In other words, following the YTD correction, Nvidia stock is currently trading as if it's 2019 again, but the prospects, in my view, look much more visible right now:

Assuming 30x in FWD earnings by the end of FY2026, and implying a premium of just 5% (coming from both the earnings surprises and upward revisions), my price target for NVDA arrives at ~$180.2/share. That's a growth potential of over 62% to the current stock price.

Risks And Your Takeaway

While I'm quite bullish on NVDA, I admit that there are a lot of risks to my thesis update. In case the revenue shift that I've discussed above doesn't lead to higher-priced Blackwell sales and the firm just keeps selling same-priced offerings that come at a higher COGS for them, the margins will continue to fall, and that will definitely hurt the stock's valuation. Also, if the price elasticity for Nvidia's demand isn't at the level I assumed, and the transferring of rising COGS doesn't happen the way I see it, it will translate to lower earnings in the medium term. Lower-than-expected earnings growth for Nvidia will mean a sharp decrease in its stock price.

Despite these obvious risks to my thesis, I believe we should be first of all assess the facts, and the odds of what's likely to happen to the operational business in the next few years. So far, I mostly see positives. Yes, the AI hype is gradually evaporating, but the demand side is strong - without Nvidia's offerings there's no chance to compete, and the big techs seem to understand it well.

Based on my valuation calculations, the stock is undervalued by over 62% (from my PT for the next 12 months), so in case you were looking for an opportunity to buy NVDA cheap, it might be your chance.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Very Bad News For Realty Income
data.description:
Earlier this month, I discussed Realty Income's (NYSE:O) latest results and noted that the company was facing growing headwinds.

Unfortunately, I have more bad news to share regarding the "monthly dividend company".

You may recall that last year, I shared an article explaining why I thought that President Trump's victory was bad news for the stock.

The reason is tariffs.

They have the potential to hurt Realty Income in two ways.

Firstly, it is expected to increase inflation, which is an issue for Realty Income because most of its leases only include fixed annual rent escalations of 1% or 1.5%. This means that the REIT's revenue will rapidly lose in purchasing power if inflation accelerates as a result of tariffs. This year, the REIT has only guided for 1.4% FFO per share growth, which will likely be quite a lot lower than the rate of inflation.

The second reason why tariffs are bad news for Realty Income is that it is heavily exposed to retail sectors that are expected to face significant pain as a result of tariffs. It already has growing tenant issues, and those have just gotten a lot bigger as a result of President Trump's latest tariff announcements.

In today's Realty Income stock review, I am going to discuss what these latest tariffs mean for the stock, and I will end by sharing my rating for the stock (Buy, Hold, or Sell) and highlighting a few of its peers that might be more attractive.

Tariffs Will Hurt Realty Income's Biggest Tenants

Earlier this month, President Trump imposed very high sweeping tariffs on most other countries in the world.

Then, more recently, he adjusted his plan by lowering the tariffs to 10% on most countries for the next 90 days while increasing the tariffs on China to a whopping 125%!

Bloomberg noted that the overall impact on the US economy should be fairly similar given that the average US tariff rate only dropped a few percentage points:

I fear that this will make Realty Income's growing tenant issues a lot worse.

As a reminder, Realty Income is heavily exposed to Walgreens (WBA), Dollar General (DG), and Dollar Tree (DLTR) today. They are its second, third, and fourth largest tenants, representing them alone about 10% of its rental revenue.

But unfortunately for Realty Income, pharmacies and dollar stores could be some of the worst-impacted retail sectors by Trump's tariff proposals:

Pharmacies have been suffering for years due to oversupply and growing competition from big players like Amazon. As a result, Walgreens (WBA), which is Realty Income's second-biggest tenant, has been closing stores left and right in an attempt to save the business.

The problem is that if your business was hurting before the tariff, then your troubles will only get a lot bigger going forward. Pharmacies import a lot of low-cost goods from countries like China, and a big segment of their clients are low-income households.

In an exclusive interview last year, the CEO of Agree Realty (ADC) predicted to us that Walgreens could eventually have to file for bankruptcy to get rid of many of its properties that are losing money. That's the only way to get out of its long-term leases.

That was before the tariffs.

Now, he is likely growing even more confident in his prediction, and before you discredit him as a fearmonger, you should note that Walgreens used to be Agree Realty's biggest tenant, so few people know this company better than they do,

If Walgreens was going to face severe losses due to the tariffs and eventually file for bankruptcy, it would undoubtedly have a significant negative impact on the market sentiment of Realty Income, given that it is one of its top tenants.

It would slow down its growth as redeveloping and releasing these properties would be costly.

Why not just sell the properties today?

Well, if it was that easy, they would probably do it, but valuations have already dropped significantly. Recently, I have come across more and more listings to buy Walgreens net lease properties at 8%+ cap rates, which is a new high.

Here is another one:

Higher cap rates result in lower property values.

Increasingly, many landlords are becoming concerned and looking to sell these properties, and this is resulting in higher cap rates. The company recently announced that it had come to a deal with private equity firm Sycamore Partners to privatize the company, which will only increase uncertainty since we won't have access to the company's financials for much longer. Also, what is the plan of this private equity firm? Are they just smelling blood and looking to break up the pieces to unlock value?

Only time will tell...

If Realty Income were to sell these properties, it would likely be dilutive since it would then need to reinvest the capital into other properties at lower cap rates.

And, unfortunately for Realty Income, it is not just pharmacies.

The REIT is also heavily exposed to dollar stores, and we have previously argued that those are the "ground zero" for tariffs. That's because they focus on lower-income households and import a lot of cheap products from China and other countries.

Their margins are already very tight, and these tariffs will make things a lot worse. Dollar Tree is particularly heavily impacted because it focuses more on discretionary goods, and it was already struggling and closing stores before the tariffs came into place. Their CEO also recently announced that he was stepping down, and one of their competitors, 99 Cents, filed for bankruptcy.

Will Dollar Tree face the same outcome?

I don't know.

But before tariffs, they were planning to close 1,000 stores in the near term, and things will not get better with tariffs.

Again, if we see more headlines of dollar stores closing down, or worse, one of these big companies filing for bankruptcy, it would likely have a very negative impact on the market sentiment of Realty Income.

Buy, Hold, or Sell?

Realty Income is facing growing tenant issues, and those will only get worse because of tariffs.

It will likely slow down its growth even further as it seeks to proactively sell weaker assets to reduce its exposure.

I fear that its growth will not keep up with inflation, leading to negative real-growth in the dividend payments.

However, the stock is already historically cheap, trading at just 12x FFO and offering a 6% dividend yield, which is compelling coming from an A-rated REIT.

Moreover, if and when we go into a recession, this could push interest rates to lower levels, benefiting net lease REITs like Realty Income.

Therefore, I still expect Realty Income to do relatively well over the long run and would give it a Hold rating.

However, I wouldn't give it a Buy rating because there are better options in its peer group.

Some of its peers enjoy better leases that offer stronger protection against inflation. As an example, W.P. Carey (WPC) and VICI Properties (VICI) both enjoy CPI-based rent escalations, which should result in faster growth in the coming years if tariffs remain in place. Despite that, they trade at fairly similar valuations as Realty Income today.

Moreover, some other peers are also less exposed to struggling retail categories like pharmacies and dollar stores. Good examples here would be Agree Realty (ADC), which focuses on stronger categories and derives most of its revenue from investment-grade-rated tenants.

So to recap: tariffs are bad news for Realty Income, and some of its peers offer better risk-to-reward given that they trade at fairly similar valuations but enjoy better leases and/or are less exposed to the weakest retail categories.

data.title: Apogee Therapeutics: Engineering Biologic Dominance
data.description:
With recent financial disclosures and pipeline updates including encouraging interim Phase 1 data for its OX40L inhibitor APG990, I believe Apogee (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:APGE) is laying the groundwork for significant value creation as a disruptor in multi-billion dollar indications, including atopic dermatitis, asthma, and beyond. This strategy focuses on optimized biologics that provide meaningful differentiation in crowded therapeutic landscapes.

Financials

Apogee's year end financials show a company laying down roots for the long haul. By December 31, 2024, cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities were at $731.1 million. This is a big jump from $395.5 million reported at the end of 2023. Most importantly, management believes this war chest will be enough to fund operating expenses through the first quarter of 2028. Having this extended cash runway is a major de-risking factor for a clinical-stage biotech - it provides time to move multiple pipeline candidates through key clinical milestones without the immediate threat of dilutive financing rounds. Having this financial strength enables the company to develop from a position of stability.

Moving an ambitious pipeline is expensive. Research and development expenses climbed to $167.9 million for the full year of 2024 from $68.4 million in 2023. This increase is not unexpected and necessary. It accurately represents the work done to date, namely the development of the lead IL-13 inhibitor APG777, combination programs APG279 (APG777 + APG990) and the APG777 + APG333 program, and the pipeline moving forward into clinical trials. The other contributors are increased personnel costs - including equity-based compensation tied to a growing research and development team - a sign of scaling operations to meet the pipeline potential.

Similarly, General and administrative expenses nearly doubled to $49.0 million in 2024 from $24.6 million in 2023. This rise reflects higher personnel costs, including equity compensation, as headcount growth was required to support expanding operations and manage the inherent challenges of a public company. Even though higher expenses drive a larger net loss, that pattern is typical of companies at the development stage.

Thus the net loss in 2024 was $182.1 million versus $84.0 million in 2023. This increased loss is caused by increased research and development, as well as general and administrative spending, which fuels pipeline advancement. This was partly offset by higher interest income - probably due to the larger cash position. While profitability is the ultimate goal, I think the current spending reflects a concentrated effort to create value through clinical development with a healthy cash position to cover short term financial risk. A runway that extends into 2028 provides some cushion for data readouts.

Pipeline Expansion

Apogee's pipeline strategy highlights its ambition to target major inflammatory diseases including atopic dermatitis, asthma and eosinophilic esophagitis in differentiated ways. Its core is APG777, an IL-13 inhibitor conceived as potentially the best in monotherapy. It is a well-validated target for Type 2 inflammation, but Apogee seeks to enhance these therapies - perhaps through optimized dosing or efficacy. This atopic dermatitis program has progressed significantly: Part A of the Phase 2 trial has been enrolled, and initial 16-week induction data are expected by mid-2025. That's a key catalyst. More long-term data from Part A (expected in 2026) and subsequent Part B data (H2 in 2026) will further define APG777's profile in this large market. Apogee is now targeting APG777 for use in asthma and eosinophilic esophagitis, with Phase 1b and Phase 2b trials starting in 2025 and data collection in 2026. In this way, the core asset is systematically expanded across relevant indications.

Apogee isn't necessarily reliant on monotherapy success, though. The company is actively exploring combination strategies aiming for first-in-class or best-in-class outcomes while recognizing the heterogeneity of diseases like atopic dermatitis. APG279 - APG777 + APG990, an OX40L inhibitor, is the most advanced combination. OX40L sits further upstream in the inflammatory cascade than IL-13, and may provide broader immunosuppression along the Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 pathways. The rationale is compelling: Combine the deep Type 2 inhibition of APG777 with the broader activity of APG990 to possibly confer superior efficacy in atopic dermatitis patients who are not responding to single-pathway inhibition. Phase 1b proof-of-concept trial of APG279 is scheduled for 2025 and will include a head-to-head comparison with the current standard-of-care DUPIXENT. Data from this important trial is expected in the second half of 2026 and will be a turning point for the company and its combination strategy.

Apogee is developing another combination combining APG777 with APG333, a TSLP inhibitor. TSLP is another key cytokine associated with inflammatory diseases, including asthma. That combined approach - now in preclinical development for asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease - extends Apogee's strategy to include targeting different disease mechanisms and possibly different patient populations. Its clinical plans are due for release in 2025. Such a multi-pronged approach, including a potentially best-in-class monotherapy and synergistic combination therapies targeting distinct pathways, positions Apogee to carve out a niche in the vast inflammatory and immunology landscape.

APG990 Initial Data Shines

More recent news has reinforced the rationale for Apogee's combination strategy, specifically for APG990. The company reported good interim data from the first-in-human Phase 1 trial of APG990, a subcutaneous, half-life-extended monoclonal antibody targeting OX40L. This trial with 40 healthy adult participants provided important pharmacokinetic and safety data.

The pharmacokinetic profile of APG990 emerges as the main finding from the interim analysis. Data revealed that the half-life of approximately 60 days applies to all tested doses. This is an encouraging result, and supports very infrequent maintenance dosing - perhaps every three or six months - with doses as low as 50 mg. The longer half-life is an integral part of Apogee's engineering strategy and a potential differentiation factor against more frequent-acting therapies. For the APG279 combination, this pharmacokinetic profile supports the use of the coformulated product in single 2 mL injections twice to four times per year. Having a convenient dosing schedule may be a competitive advantage in terms of patient adherence and preference.

Equally important was the tolerability profile from the Phase 1 study. APG990 was tolerated well across five cohorts at doses up to 1,200mg. Like in any trial, treatment-emergent adverse events were observed mainly due to headaches (>=10% incidence). In total, 53% of participants reported at least one treatment-emergent adverse event. Critically, however, no Grade 3 treatment-emergent adverse events related to the study drug were recorded, no severe adverse events, and no adverse events led to study discontinuation. Neither pyrexia nor chills, which are sometimes associated with immune-modulating therapies, were observed. A clean initial safety profile is necessary even for drugs indicated for chronic conditions and as part of a combination therapy.

Apogee leaders understandably stressed the importance of those findings. CEO Michael Henderson noted the extended pharmacokinetic profile and good tolerability that support APG990 as a combination partner with APG777 for atopic dermatitis and other inflammatory diseases. He noted that APG279 may broadly inhibit Type 1, 2, and 3 inflammation, providing possibly greater efficacy with fewer side effects than other therapies. Supporting preclinical toxicology studies with no safety finding, even at 150 mg/kg per agent over 3 months, and potential for enhanced pharmacologic responses supports the case for APG279. Those encouraging results led Henderson to confirm the plan to begin the head-to-head Phase 1b trial of APG279 against DUPIXENT later this year.

The potential for quarterly or less frequent dosing was mentioned, and the therapeutic hypothesis was reiterated. Combining APG990's broad inhibition with APG777's deep Type 2 inhibition may result in an effective but safe treatment approach. These good interim results support the APG990 in the pipeline and de-risk the APG279 combination trial. Now the company plans to file an Investigational New Drug application or its foreign equivalent for APG279, with a Phase 1b trial starting in 2025 and data due in the second half of 2026.

Risks and Competition

The real risk lies in the clinical development itself. Good early-stage data, such as APG990, does not guarantee success in larger, longer Phase 2 or Phase 3 trials. Strange safety signals could emerge, or the drugs may fail to demonstrate statistical significance compared with placebo or active comparators such as DUPIXENT. Regulatory hurdles also pose a risk. FDA and other global health authorities require robust data packages meeting high standards to obtain approval. Common industry delays in trial enrollment, data analysis, or regulatory review can impact timelines and investor sentiment.

Competitive risk is high in inflammatory and immunology. Markets like atopic dermatitis and asthma are stuffed with established players as well as many therapies in development. DUPIXENT represents a major commercial success, and the benchmark Apogee seeks to challenge with APG777 monotherapy and APG279 combination. Other companies develop novel IL-13 inhibitors, OX40/OX40L inhibitors, TSLP inhibitors and other mechanisms. Apogee's differentiation strategy, including extended half-life and infrequent dosing, is critical for competing effectively, but success is dependent on showing clear advantages in clinical trials.

Execution risk is another factor. Managing multiple clinical programs across different indications and development stages requires significant operational expertise. Apogee needs to successfully execute its clinical trials, manage its supply chain and navigate the regulatory landscape to meet its stated timelines. The cash runway is strong now but significant additional capital will be needed for development, possible Phase 3 trials and eventual commercialization. Downstream challenges are also related to market access and reimbursement negotiations. Investors must weigh those risks against the considerable potential market opportunity if Apogee's differentiated candidates prove successful.

Market Valuation And Upside Outlook

The opportunity for atopic dermatitis alone is huge - there are about 26 million U.S. patients and over 100 million worldwide. About 30% have moderate-to-severe disease, which translates to about 7.8 million U.S. patients eligible for biologics. Given that the treatment costs for agents like DUPIXENT range from $35,000-$40,000 per year, a 10% U.S. share for APG777 or APG279 could translate to $2.7-$3.1 billion in revenue. Internationally, assuming 20 million eligible patients and 5% penetration at $30,000 per year, adds another $3 billion, making atopic dermatitis a $6 billion total addressable market under conservative assumptions.

Asthma has additional upside: There are 25 million U.S. patients - 5-10% with severe, uncontrolled disease - 1.25 to 2.5 million. The APG777 + APG333 combo could bring in around $1.8 billion domestically at similar treatment costs, with global expansion reaching $3.5-$4 billion.

Eosinophilic esophagitis, though smaller, is still meaningful. This market is worth more than $6 billion worldwide, with around 180,000 U.S. patients and biologic pricing near $35,000. A small penetration here could generate hundreds of millions annually.

Together, these indications represent a market potential of more than $13 billion annually, excluding future expansion into related diseases or regions. Differentiated biologics with infrequent dosing and broad efficacy could command premium share and pricing for Apogee. Long-duration dosing also provides convenience and a competitive edge.

At around $1.9 billion, Apogee's valuation only captures a small slice of the long-term upside potential. A longer cash runway to 2028, plus a number of near-term catalysts including a critical Phase 2 readout for APG777 support a strong upside case. Apogee isn’t just advancing therapies, but is positioning to lead in the multi-billion dollar inflammatory market.

data.title: Amazon's Retail May Be Vulnerable - Its Cloud Business Is Not
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As a result of Trump's tariffs on China, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is canceling import orders from Southeast Asia and China.

In a move seen as cushioning the impact from U.S. trade policy, Amazon (AMZN) has cancelled orders that originate from China and Southeast Asian countries, leaving certain vendors on the hook with unsold inventory.

At first, this sounds pretty bad. It looks like Amazon is cutting back or that something is fundamentally wrong with the commercial segment of its business. But I see it in a slightly different way. Amazon isn't pulling back because of weak demand or operational issues. The company is adapting fast to the changing environment, and this is an advantage. Amazon is flexible, it is adapting, and it's protecting itself from the unpredictable geopolitical environment. This is the behavior I want to see from the company I invest in.

Without a doubt, the trade war with China and the constant changes in policies create short-term headwinds for Amazon, but I believe the long-term future of Amazon is in AWS. This segment is growing rapidly and showing incredible margin expansion. Commercial is a huge part of Amazon, of course, but the cloud is a real gem.

I do not exclude the possibility of a pullback in the stock price before the growth, and I view every pullback as an opportunity to add more, as long as the business of the company remains strong. In fact, I added more shares to my position early this week and plan to constantly increase this position, even though the valuation is a premium.

For me, personally, Amazon is a Strong Buy. And this is one of a few US tech companies I am ready to go into recession with.

Is the future AWS?

Retail remains the largest part of Amazon's revenue base, but AWS delivers the most profits. I've compared Net Sales (TTM) for Amazon in 2023 and 2024, you can see the comparison below in the picture. Commercial in North America drives the same 61% of the Net Sales for the company both in 2023 and 2024, but AWS continues to surprise investors in its own way.

In Q4 2024 alone, AWS generated $28.8 billion in revenue, growing 19% year-over-year, which is an incredible growth, but it can't justify the premium valuation of the stock of this company. What can, is its operating income. So, operating income in the trailing twelve months was $68.5 billion, up 86% year over year. An expansion this rapid in operating income is something unseen for a company of this size, and the reason for this expansion is AWS.

AWS is just 17% of total revenue, yet it generates more than half of Amazon's total operating income. Out of $68.5 billion, $39.8 billion was made from AWS. I must repeat it again: 17% of revenue drives 58% of profits. I am amazed by these numbers, although I am aware that still a huge part of the revenue comes from other segments, which we can't rule out, and Amazon is working hard to sustain those incomes.

AWS is such a big deal, and everyone talks about it because this isn't a one-time acceleration. This is a consistent trend, showing that AWS is becoming more profitable, more rooted, and more important to the tech industry quarter after quarter.

But numbers alone can't fully explain my point of view on Amazon. In general, I have a pretty personal approach to investing and choosing stocks. I tend to invest either in companies that I used and liked or the companies, business of which I find fascinating or unique (of course, I check the fundamentals as well).

Amazon and AWS are both. The majority of people in my surroundings are from the high-tech industry, and I can say that the demand for engineers who can integrate and manage AWS services has steadily grown over the past few years. If seven to eight years ago an AWS certificate was nice to have and some companies asked for it, now when looking for a job, "cloud experience" is a part of most requirements for the candidates, and when someone says "cloud" in high-tech, they mean AWS.

I recently checked U.S. job postings on Glassdoor and found around 41,800 software engineering positions open. Funny enough, the search for AWS engineer gave 22,700 positions. That's over half. AWS isn't just a desirable skill anymore - it's an industry standard, and the industry depends on it.

Backing this up further, research from AWSforEngineers.com shows that 76% of employers actively factor AWS certification into hiring decisions, and 60% prefer certified candidates. AWS-certified professionals are earning between $140K and $200K, and demand for this talent is expected to grow by 30% annually through 2025.

Getting an AWS certification can boost your pay. Studies show that AWS certified professionals often earn 25-30% more than those without certifications. This increase can change based on where you work, your industry, and how much experience you have.

This line of thought made me question the ability of AWS to progress. If everyone is using it already, what are the catalysts for growth? Besides the organic growth of the industry brought by the reliance of many businesses on AWS infrastructure, we must include the growth of the AI sector and everything related to it. Amazon is actively adding the number of solutions in areas like artificial intelligence and quantum computing (yes, actual commercial quantum computing through Amazon Braket), the use cases of which are only widening.

So, AWS not only captured a huge market share of modern companies, but it's already positioned itself to benefit and to profit from the next generation of innovation. Just this Thursday, Amazon CEO Andy Jassy reiterated that AI is one of the company's biggest priorities and investment areas. He made it clear that Amazon wants to be established in the middle of the AI boom, especially through AWS. Given the amazing reputation of the company and the strength of its branding, I have no reason to think AMZN won't be successful in capturing market share further on, including in the AI industry.

Commercial Still Matters, and It's at Risk

Retail is still Amazon's biggest segment, and I don't want to understate its importance. In Q4, 2024, Amazon made $75.5 billion from its online stores and another $47.5 billion from third-party seller services. These numbers are massive. This side of the business supports the whole Amazon ecosystem, including AWS, and thanks to this part of the business, the brand is one of the most well-known.

But it's also the segment most vulnerable right now.

If Trump continues escalating tariffs on China, as he just did, raising them to 125%, we could see a significant disruption in Amazon's retail supply chain. A huge portion of Amazon's retail products are supplied by Chinese vendors, and they're under pressure like never before. In fact, the head of China's largest e-commerce association told Reuters:

This isn't just a tax issue, it's that the entire cost structure gets entirely overwhelmed. It'll be very hard for anyone to survive in the U.S. market.

Vendors are already preparing to either raise prices or exit the U.S. market entirely. Amazon's cancellations of import orders were likely a proactive response to this exact pressure. If these tariffs stay or, worse, rise further, Amazon's retail segment will be hit by it.

That said, Amazon's strength lies in its ability to adapt. Even while commercial is under pressure, the company continues to drive growth in its higher-margin segments. Besides AWS, there is also the advertising segment, which is also growing and expanding its margins. So, my outlook is this: Amazon will be under pressure short term, but it won't damage the long-term outcome. I am planning on holding this stock for more than a couple of years at least, so I am reacting to the current turmoil.

Valuation

Amazon stock is definitely not cheap, some might even call it expensive. The company trades at a forward P/E of around 30, while the sector median is ~14. The funny thing is that you probably won't ever get AWS growth, margins, and global infrastructure scale at a discount. You can be sure of that after looking at the PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth). In the case of companies like Amazon, there is simply no reason to look at the P/E. You are paying much more for the growth the company makes. By the FWD PEG ratio, the company is only ~12% more expensive than the sector median, but if we look at the trailing twelve months, the company is ~40% undervalued. I know it sounds weird, but Amazon is both overvalued and undervalued at the same time. How can it be? Well, this is very simple. All of the valuation metrics we use are relative. And when there are no companies to compare our company to, this is the result we get.

The truth is, if we take two investors, one that claims that AMZN is overvalued, and it's time to sell, and another one that claims that the stock is undervalued, and it's time to buy, both will be right if their assumptions make sense. This is the beauty of evaluating the company by yourself. Understand what you expect will happen, write down those assumptions, and then make up your mind. The only detail, you must be ready to put money on your decision!

Conclusion

I know there's a lot going on right now, and it's easy to get distracted by the headlines: trade wars, vendor cancellations, and even valuation debates. But when I zoom out and look at the business itself, not just the stock, I see a company that has rapid growth and is progressing constantly. Amazon isn't perfect, and yes, retail will face pressure, especially if Trump continues down this tariff road. But Amazon isn't just a retailer anymore. It's a tech company with one of the most profitable cloud infrastructures in the world and big AI plans.

I can't time the market, and with Amazon, I'm not trying to. Maybe the stock will pull back again. If it will, I will add more. I like the direction, I see that management is ready to adjust to any conditions, which I truly admire. The fundamentals are great, the valuation is okay, and I believe in the long-term success of this company. For me, Amazon is one of those rare companies I'm willing to hold through uncertainty and even through a recession. That's why I bought more this week. And that's why I'll probably do it again. The rating is Strong Buy.

data.title: LSI Industries: Enlightened
data.description:
Shares of LSI Industries (NASDAQ:LYTS) have seen the light in recent years, as the vertically integrated lighting solution provider has seen margin expansion, growth in adjacent markets and has shown real momentum, that is the business and its shares.

This has pushed share upwards, as the company embarks on interesting bolt-on deals, all of which makes me frankly upbeat on the business here and considering getting involved in the near term.

Integrated Lightning & Display Solutions

LSI Industries is a vertically integrated commercial indoor/outdoor lightning and display solutions business, which has a strong foundation and growth profile in vertical markets.

The company deliberately focuses on integrated solutions: those including design, production and installation, as this is a distinguishing feature, as claimed by the business.

Most of the business is tied to lightning solutions and within this segment the company focuses on indoor, outdoor and control solutions. This is complemented by display solutions and related services. Typical customer applications include automotive, fueling, quick-service restaurants, retail & grocery, parking, sport complexes and warehouses.

While core expertise is seen in lighting, the company is looking to expand into adjacent markets as well, these including safety & security, solar, infrastructure, and others.

The business focuses on organic achievements and bolt-on M&A efforts, as the company has grown sales from about $300 million a decade ago, to half a billion now. The company posted very small margins in the latter 2010s, at times essentially breaking-even, as operating margins have improved to about 7% of sales in more recent years. However, the company has huge ambitions, as it hopes to expand sales to $800 million by 2028, to be accompanied by continued margin expansion as well.

The Base Case

In August of last year, LSI reported its fiscal 2024 results, with revenues down 6% to $469.8 million, after a tough comparison in the year before. GAAP operating profits were down minimally to $35.5 million, as GAAP earnings of $25.0 million translated into earnings of $0.83 per share, down five cents on the year before. Adjusted earnings fell by four cents to $0.95 per share, yet most of the discrepancy was related to long-term performance-based compensation expenses, as realistic earnings likely trend closer to $0.85 per share.

The company reported a $51 million EBITDA number, in line with the net debt load of the business, for a leverage ratio of 1.0 times. This net debt load came in to existence after a $50 million acquisition of EMI Industries, a deal which closed in April 2024. With an $87 million sales contribution, the deal would become a huge growth driver to 2025.

As mentioned above, the company has seen improved results post-pandemic after margins were dismal in the 2010s. A $15 stock in the early 2000s fell to just $3 in 2019 as the business was really struggling to post a profit, but shares recovered to the $15 mark in 2024. Based on the earnings power of $0.85 per share, as discussed above, shares commanded a market multiple at those levels.

Moving Ahead

Supported by the purchase of EMI, LSI reported a 12% increase in first quarter sales to $138 million in November. That was about the good news, as adjusted earnings were reported down six cents to $0.26 per share.

Growth accelerated to 36% in the second quarter, with revenues advancing to $148 million, supported by a very sound 14% organic growth rate. This was driven by display growth, notably in grocery stores, and in particular was driven by catch-up demand. The broken merger of Kroger (KR) with Albertson's (ACI) caused both parties to catch up on deferred investments into the business, as this drove momentum in the quarter.

The sound sales numbers, were not accompanied by margin progress, with adjusted earnings up a mere two pennies to $0.26 per share. So far this year, operating margins (GAAP) have been down nearly two points to 6% and change.

With 30 million shares trading at $16, although they briefly hit the $25 mark upon the earnings release, LSI currently commands at $480 million equity valuation, as the enterprise valuation comes in just over half a billion here.

With revenues trending near $600 million per annum here, the business trades at less than 1 times sales and with adjusted earnings seen topping a dollar per share, and realistic earnings seen around $0.90 per share, shares still trade at a market multiple.

Another Bolt-On Deal

In early March, LSI announced that it has acquired Canada's Best Holdings in a deal valued at $24 million in cash, this number excluding a $7 million potential earn-out.

The headline purchase price is valued at 1 times sales of $24 million, largely in line with the own valuation, and in fact a small premium to LSI´s own valuation here. This is for good reasons, as a $4.0 million EBITDA contribution works down to margins equal to 16% and change, comparing to just 9% for LSI itself.

Pro forma net debt is seen at 1.1 times EBITDA, still very manageable, as the business will see sales grow by around 4-5% on a pro forma basis, and might see a high single digit contribution to EBITDA. This makes that I am happy to work with realistic earnings around a dollar per share, despite the fact that margins are down two points so far this year. Every point in margin expansion from here could grow earnings by around fifteen cents here, as this could easily unleash real upside from here.

All this makes me rather upbeat at $16 and change. Shares trade at 16 times earnings, while earnings might see a modest jump from the purchase of Canada’s Best, as well as benefit from margin expansion. This makes that I can easily see a $1.50 earnings per share number in a few years down the road, which, if achieved, could reveal real upside here.

This makes me rather upbeat about this smaller name, looking to keep a close eye on the developments from here, and looking to get involved on dips here.

data.title: Weekly Market Pulse: The End Of The Beginning
data.description:
The stock market rallied last week, the S&P 500 up nearly 6%, spurred on by the suspension of some of the tariffs imposed on April 2nd. The “reciprocal” part of the new tariffs was suspended for 90 days, while the 10% universal tariff was retained. Tariffs on China, however, were raised further after they retaliated against President Trump’s previous retaliation for China’s earlier response to the initial tariffs, which were added on to tariffs from Trump’s first term and the Biden administration. Or at least I think so. There is some confusion about whether some countries even have the 10% universal tariff and whether it will be added to the 25% tariffs already imposed on Mexico and Canada (I don’t think so, but who knows?). Just to further confuse things, the administration published a list of exemptions late Friday, after the market closed, for smartphones, laptops, memory chips, and other electronic items from some or maybe all of the tariffs. However, Howard Lutnick, the Commerce Secretary, said on Sunday that those items were only exempted because they’ll soon get their own set of specific tariffs, probably on the semiconductors they all contain. Maybe in a month or two. Or not, depending on how an investigation ordered by President Trump comes out.

Stocks may rally on Monday when the market opens, based on the exemptions, but I don’t know that I’d bet much on it sticking. The administration’s tariff policies are obviously not well-thought-out and the unintended consequences were either underestimated or, more likely, not considered at all. A few months ago, the administration crowed about Apple (AAPL) promising to invest $500 billion in US-based manufacturing, but then threatened to devastate their profits with tariffs on China and India. How do they expect Apple to invest in US manufacturing if they don’t have the income to do so? The same could be said for the entire reshoring project. By imposing tariffs before companies have had a chance to build new US capacity, they’ve made reshoring more expensive and even less likely to happen. A trade war is more likely to reduce investment in the US unless we simultaneously and drastically reduce our budget deficit. Reducing imports will mean reducing exports too because other countries won’t have the dollars with which to buy them. If we don’t trade with them, they don’t invest.

Last week’s stock market rally was accompanied by more negative moves in other markets. Gold was up more on the week than stocks and continued to climb even after stocks started to recover. The 10-year Treasury note yield rose 50 basis points last week, while the 2-year note rose 32; term spreads widened. High yield credit spreads narrowed last week but only by a few basis points. Maybe most disturbing of all, the dollar fell over 3% as foreign investors took flight (which may be part of what was ailing the bond market too). The US, as I said above, needs foreign investment to fund our savings shortfall. With news last week from Elon Musk that after weeks of disruption they’ve managed to find a grand total of $150 billion of fraud, waste and abuse, that need does not seem likely to wane anytime soon. If foreign investors decide to pick up their capital and go home, we have a rather large problem.

Foreign investors own about 20% of US stocks, 30% of US Treasuries, and 30% of corporate credit. Official accounts of other countries may stick with US Treasuries (bidding at Treasury auctions last week seemed fairly normal) but individual and institutional investors may feel differently. A Euro-denominated investor who bought a 10-year Treasury note at the beginning of this year would be about breakeven on the price and would have accrued a quarter’s worth of interest, but they’d be down about 10% on the change in the Euro/Dollar exchange rate. If that math continues for very long, the rise in rates and the drop in the dollar are likely to accelerate. The same is true of US stocks, especially the growth stock-heavy S&P 500 where a lot of that foreign equity money is sitting. Stocks may have come off the mat last week, but they aren’t even back to the levels of “Liberation Day” eve. Just getting back to there would require a further 5.7% rally from Friday’s close. And the index is also still down 8.6% YTD and 12.6% since the February peak.

The chaos of the tariff rollout has created a level of uncertainty that makes investment in the US very difficult, maybe impossible, to justify. Policies change on a whim with seemingly little thought to the consequences. The exemptions granted last Friday seem good at first, but consider that a part used in a smartphone – say a battery – will continue to face the full tariff but an imported phone that contains that battery will be exempt. How exactly will that incentivize investment in the US? The granting of exemptions also creates, at a minimum, the perception of favoritism if not outright corruption. The big tech companies who donated to President Trump’s inauguration fund or his campaign get an exemption for their products, but what about the thousands of small and medium-sized businesses that don’t have access? What about the lower margin US-based manufacturer who relies on some critical input from China who will face the full force of Trump’s China tariffs? Do they get invited to the White House? Or will they be shuffled off to the Treasury Secretary? Or relegated to some lower level official who has no more chance of getting the White House’s attention than the petitioner?

The universal and “reciprocal” tariffs also make it harder, not easier, to sign trade deals. The universal 10% and “reciprocal” tariffs abrogated all of the free trade agreements the US had in place, demonstrating to the entire world that we do not abide by our agreements. Who wants to sign a trade deal with the country that just violated numerous trade deals? One of the reasons the rest of the world invests in the US is because we respect the rule of law, with agreements signed by previous administrations honored by the new one. Further, contrary to President Trump and Secretary Bessent’s poker analogies, the US doesn’t have a very good hand right now. As we just found out with the exemptions announcement, there are certain things we want – and need – in the US that we can’t produce ourselves and that is unlikely to change for a long time, if ever.

Treasury Secretary Bessent recently said that Chinese tariffs on the US don’t matter because they don’t buy anything from us. That is true, and also exactly why Bessent is wrong about the hand he holds. If China loses all its US export sales, it hits them to the tune of about 2% of their GDP. The loss of US exports to China is trivial compared to the size of our economy, but a lot of the things we import from China are critical inputs to our economy and will affect a lot more than 2% of GDP. For a lot of companies, particularly small and medium-sized ones, continued high Chinese tariffs are a death knell. China’s loss, on the other hand, is just money, and they have a very high savings rate. They can try to open other markets to replace US sales, they can use fiscal stimulus to soften the blow of the loss, and they can spread the pain across the entire economy. Which country do you think will better tolerate the hardships that result from a trade war?

Consumer and corporate sentiment is already negative and falling fast. The preliminary University of Michigan’s Consumer Sentiment Survey for April, released last week, was the second-lowest level since 1952, when we were still in the middle of the Korean War:

Consumers report multiple warning signs that raise the risk of recession: expectations for business conditions, personal finances, incomes, inflation, and labor markets all continued to deteriorate this month. The share of consumers expecting unemployment to rise in the year ahead increased for the fifth consecutive month and is now more than double the November 2024 reading and the highest since 2009.

Year-ahead inflation expectations surged from 5.0% last month to 6.7% this month, the highest reading since 1981 and marking four consecutive months of unusually large increases of 0.5 percentage points or more. This month’s rise was seen across all three political affiliations. Long-run inflation expectations climbed from 4.1% in March to 4.4% in April, reflecting a particularly large jump among independents.

The reprieve offered last week by President Trump just further complicates the economic outlook. The delay is for 90 days, but how many actual trade agreements can the administration negotiate in that time? Will the deadline be extended? How long will the exemptions announced last Friday last? The President has recently renewed this threat to put tariffs on pharmaceuticals. When will that be announced and how high will the tariffs be? The future is no less uncertain now – and maybe more so – than it was at the beginning of last week.

I hope – and expect – to see more changes to the tariff plan in coming days and weeks. Best would be to call the whole thing off and concentrate on policies that offer greater incentives to companies – US and foreign – to invest in the US. They will be reluctant to do that with interest rates rising and the dollar falling. If that continues, more alterations to the tariffs seem almost certain. The trade war likely has a long way to go but the tactical retreat by the Trump administration last week was probably the end of the beginning phase. What comes next? In the age of Trump no one knows the answer to that question.

Environment

The dollar is now down over the last three years and at the very bottom of its recent range. Currently at about 100, the dollar index is on the verge of falling through support that has held for the last three years. A breakdown through 99 would likely beget a lot more selling, with a further 10% correction to 89-90 likely in coming months. We are in a short-term downtrend and if we do break lower here, the 115 peak in 2022 will likely be seen in the future as a generational top. I suspect we are entering a weak dollar period that will last a long time. This loss of confidence in the US will be very hard to repair.

The 10-year Treasury yield surged last week and appears to be aimed directly toward 5%. Rates that high would do irreparable harm to the Trump agenda. Remember, Treasury Secretary Bessent said he and the president were focused on the 10-year yield. Well, it isn’t going where they want it to go, and it won’t without a change of plan.

Markets

The focus has been on the S&P 500 in this selloff but small cap stocks have taken this the hardest. It shouldn’t be lost on investors that small caps came into the year with a considerable valuation advantage over large cap. That hasn’t mattered one bit.

The falling dollar does have one silver lining – international stocks. Latin America and Europe are both up over 5% this year, and the broader international index is slightly positive. This is what we expect with a weak dollar, but it isn’t always the case that better-than-the-US means profitable. A global recession wouldn’t be good for any country’s stock market although a falling dollar might still keep non-US stocks outperforming by falling less in dollar terms.

Take a look this week at the 3-year returns in the chart below. The S&P 500 traded down 20% at one point last week but didn’t close there, so according to some, that isn’t a bear market – yet. But if you think it was – and I do – then this is the second bear market in a little over 3 years and the 3rd in 5 years. We haven’t seen that since the 1930s, so congratulations if you survived it.

Sectors

Consumer staples and utilities are the lone winners this year, with technology and consumer discretionary bringing up the rear. I’d start looking at the 3-year laggards for potential buys. The worst performers are materials, real estate, energy, and healthcare.

Economy/Market Indicators

Credit spreads are still at the top of my worry list, but they did improve a little last week.

Economy/Economic Data

  • Small business optimism fell, but is still up considerably over the last year.
  • Inflation was less than expected, with headline CPI and PPI both negative month to month. Don’t expect that to continue.
  • Unemployment claims are, remarkably, still only at 224k. Again, don’t expect it to stay that way. We’ll start to see layoffs – government and private sector – hit those numbers soon.
  • The consumer sentiment numbers were pretty awful and, I think, more importantly crossed all political lines. Democrats, independents and Republicans all reported a more dour mood. Being upset with the current economic situation is a bipartisan affair.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Starwood Property: Hefty Yield And 11% Discount Make It A Buy
data.description:
The market rollercoaster continues and while that has rattled passive investors who are focused on capital gains in index funds such as the S&P 500 (SPY), it’s also created opportunities for income and value investors who are looking to pick up bargains. Such appears to be the case now with Starwood Property Trust (NYSE:STWD). At the current price of $17.82, STWD trades close its 52-week low and carries a 10.9% dividend yield, as shown below.

I last covered STWD back in November 2024, highlighting its strategic shift away from office buildings in favor of growing its infrastructure investments. The stock was already trading at a discount to its undepreciated book value at that time, and has since fallen by 10.5%. Thanks to dividends, its total return of -6% still out-performed the 11% decline in SPY over the same timeframe. In this article, I revisit STWD and discuss why the bigger discount and high yield makes STWD an attractive high income ‘Buy’ at present, so let’s get started!

Why STWD?

Starwood Property Trust is an externally managed commercial mortgage REIT goes beyond just traditional commercial loans. It takes a multicylinder approach toward investments where opportunities lay. The U.S. office represents just 10% of STWD’s total portfolio, and commercial loans make up just over half (54%) of the total. As shown below, residential lending, owned properties, infrastructure lending make up another 34% of the portfolio total.

STWD’s multipronged approach toward investments helps it to be agile and focus the portfolio on growth areas while de-risking away from lesser attractive ones. As shown below, cyclical sectors such as office and hotels have declined in their overall weightings in the commercial loan portfolio, while more durable segments like industrial and multifamily properties have grown since 2020.

Meanwhile, STWD delivered distributable EPS of $0.48 during Q4 2024, which fully covered the $0.48 quarterly dividend. For the full year 2024, STWD distributable EPS was $2.02, which more than covered the $1.92 annual dividend rate. Notably, STWD has paid an uninterrupted dividend since 2009 (when it became public) and has consistently held the $0.48 quarterly dividend rate since 2014.

STWD had robust investment activity last year, with $5.1 billion of new investments. Notably, most of the investments (67%) were in segments other than commercial lending. This included $2.6 billion worth of infrastructure lending last year. This was driven by increased investment opportunities in energy and data centers, which are areas expected to expand significantly over the next decade and beyond. Both energy and data center are inter-related, as the latter sector drives growth in the former. According to McKinsey, demand for data center capacity by measure of gigawatts is expected to grow at a 22% CAGR through 2030.

At the same time, power consumption by data centers is expected to grow at a 9% CAGR through the end of this decade, with Hyperscalers and Co-location companies driving much of that growth, as shown below.

STWD’s property segment also saw significant unrealized gain in fair value due to strong NOI growth of 9% in the Florida affordable multifamily portfolio. Management expects for rents to increase again this year once the Department of Housing and Urban Development releases its maximum rent levels this quarter.

At the same time, STWD has made solid progress on its REO (real estate owned) properties. These are buildings on which the borrower had defaulted on their loan obligations and have been foreclosed by STWD. Recent resolutions included the sale of a Portland multifamily asset that enabled STWD to realize its full-cost basis of $61 million. STWD also collected insurance proceeds on a property affected by a 2020 California wildfire, with proceeds of $1 million in excess of cost basis.

Management is guiding for robust lending activity this year, with expectations that more loans will be originated in 2025 than any year since 2021. This includes the targeting of high-quality property mortgages with low loan-to-value ratios. STWD’s property segment could also be a beneficiary of tariffs, as that increases the cost of lumber and other construction materials, thereby making existing properties more valuable and in demand. Plus, as noted earlier, STWD has continued opportunities to pivot its investment portfolio toward infrastructure lending due to tailwinds in the data center and energy sectors.

STWD’s growth is supported by a stronger balance sheet with a debt-to-equity ratio of 2.1x, sitting below the ~2.5x level from recent years. In addition, STWD carries $1.8 billion in total liquidity, giving it the flexibility to make opportunistic investments and increase leverage should opportunities arise.

Lastly, I find STWD to be highly appealing at the current price of $17.82 with a price-to-undepreciated book value ratio of 0.89x, equating to an 11% discount. STWD’s undepreciated book value of $19.94 already bakes in a CECL reserve balance of $482 million (an increase of $36 million sequentially), which represents 4.6% of the lending and REO portfolio. As such, the book value already bakes known portfolio headwinds as of last review.

Risks to STWD include headwinds to the office sector due to government downsizing that’s taken place since the start of the year. It’s worth noting, however, that STWD has optionality with respect to office buildings, as reflected by management comments on an office foreclosure in Washington, D.C. during the last earnings call:

We are beginning interior demolition on a $115 million office building we foreclosed on in D.C. and are converting it into a beautiful multifamily, which you will see on the cover of our supplemental. Rather than sell this asset in a depressed DC office market, our underwriting has us returning a gain to shareholders upon completion. We have resolved or modified 25 assets totaling $2.8 billion to date with 12 modifications and 12 assets we have taken into REO to reposition or sell as we did in the previous example. With markets repairing, we expect the pace of resolutions to pick up going forward.

Other risks include potentially unknown future foreclosures. This includes pockets of weakness exist as it relates to federal workforce downsizing and pressures on life science due to federal funding cuts. This could negatively affect STWD’s property collateral that are directly or indirectly impacted by those trends, such as a life science property loan in Boston that is currently going through leasing motions.

Investor Takeaway

Starwood Property Trust presents a compelling opportunity for income-focused investors after the recent dip in price. It carries a high 10.8% dividend yield and trades at an 11% discount to its undepreciated book value. Despite broader market volatility and a pullback in the stock price, STWD has maintained dividend coverage. It’s also diversified its portfolio away from cyclical office exposure, and leaned into growth sectors like infrastructure, energy, and data centers.

Plus, STWD carries a stronger balance sheet, ample liquidity, and proactive repositioning of underperforming assets. This makes it well-positioned to capitalize on market dislocations while continuing to deliver attractive income to investors.

data.title: 2 REIT-Focused ETFs Delivering Investors Monthly Cash Flow
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

We recently went over two closed-end funds that provide exposure to real estate investment trusts. That included Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) and Neuberger Berman Real Estate Securities Income Fund (NRO). Today, I wanted to look at two different exchange-traded funds ("ETFs") that also provide REIT exposure. However, these funds aren't leveraged—instead, they come up with other ways to provide significantly higher distribution rates to investors and pay that cash flow out monthly.

Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield ETF (RIET)

  • Distribution Frequency: Monthly
  • Distribution Yield: 10.30% (SEC yield 8.74%)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.50%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $88.37 million
  • Structure: Passive ETF

RIET's investment objective:

...is to track the performance, before fees and expenses of the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index, a rules-based index designed to provide diversified exposure to 100 U.S.-list that's estate-related securities that collectively provide income through high dividend yields.

Several key features of RIET include;

  • access to 100 select real estate securities paying high dividend yields
  • "smooth" monthly distributions for shareholders
  • real estate specialist Hoya Capital Real Estate is behind the fund
  • an inflation hedge, which we all know is essential at this time.

This is one I've held for a while now, shortly after the launch of the fund. However, the fund launched at a fairly poor time for REIT exposure when the Fed began raising rates aggressively. That caused significant downward pressure on the fund, and it rather quickly fell around a third in terms of its share price. That said, I personally averaged down aggressively through at the lower prices and have an unrealized gain.

Over this time, I've also been collecting the attractive monthly distribution, which has been quite steady as well. They even raised it in 2023 and haven't cut it despite a few of their underlying positions cutting their own payouts.

With a solid distribution rate of just over 10%, one should clearly be curious where that comes from. The fund itself isn't leveraged to get to those sorts of yields, but instead, they invest in more mREIT exposure—these tend to be higher-yielding investments due to their substantial leverage. REITs, in general, are relatively highly leveraged instruments and then mREITs take it even further.

The current top ten shows us that Rithm Capital Corp. (RITM), Annaly Capital Management (NLY) and Starwood Property Trust (STWD) are the largest mREIT holdings currently. NLY recently raised its dividend, and STWD has been rather resilient for an mREIT. However, the general trend is quite clear that most mREITs tend to cut their high dividends over time rather than raise them.

Here are some other examples of RIET's mREIT holdings that aren't in the top ten but are in the portfolio. The trend here is more dividend cuts over time, as we would expect. Some have even been cut quite recently, though DX is another one of those exceptions where they recently raised their payout.

With that, these come with quite high distribution rates, and it's why RIET can also pay higher rates to its investors.

NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI)

  • Distribution Frequency: Monthly
  • Distribution Yield: 12.19% (SEC yield 3.16%)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.68%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $28.2 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

IYRI's investment objective is to "generate high monthly income with the potential for equity appreciation." To achieve this, the fund will "distribute high levels of monthly income through a data-driven call option strategy on ETFs that seek to track the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index." The fund is also actively managed, which makes it more flexible than taking a simple indexed approach. The Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index, in this case, is just the reference index, which the underlying portfolio may or may not mirror exactly.

This is a newer fund and is quite small at this time, but it is also the first of its kind that I've seen. Right in the investment policy is how the fund can offer its relatively higher distribution rate, which is through utilizing a call option strategy. I've not seen another fund offer a call writing strategy that is specifically focused on REITs previously. This is primarily where the active part of the portfolio comes from. I don't currently own this fund, but it is on my watchlist of potential ideas.

When it comes to writing options, they are given quite a bit of flexibility as well, noting that they primarily will be writing covered call options on the index or ETFs. They hold individual names, so while it technically wouldn't be covered calls directly because you can't hold an index, and they don't hold the ETF, the underlying portfolio will still primarily reflect the index/ETF. So, indirectly, they are essentially writing covered calls.

They even further allow for the potential to employ an options call spread strategy. In that case, they are buying and selling calls simultaneously. They still look to generate a net-credit from the call spread, but with the long call, it can potentially allow for some further upside participation. Which is to counteract one of the downsides of a call writing strategy where upside participation can be capped if the underlying portfolio is rising too quickly.

As a newer fund, they only have a few distributions under their belt so far.

For its top ten holdings, it is primarily the larger REIT names that we would expect and that a lot of REIT investors are probably more familiar with.

Given these types of holdings, we can see why the 30-day SEC yield comes to around 3%. It is the fund's option strategy that brings it up closer to the 12% payout. The SEC yield would be factoring in the income generated from the dividend yield alone, not the derivatives strategy it employs and the subsequent gains/losses it generates from that.

This is also unlike RIET; with its focus on looking for higher-yielding instruments, we see some more unique names and a higher SEC yield. That isn't a bad thing either, it is just different. In fact, it can make a combination of RIET and IYRI quite appropriate, given their differentiated approaches. With IYRI's call writing strategy, it also changes it from other REIT-focused funds, so it could be appropriate for more high-income-focused investors or in combination with other REIT funds or portfolios.

data.title: Tax Free Municipal Bonds For A Safer Retirement: DMB
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

I don't know a single person who truly loves taxes. Some say the exception may be government officials. There are suggestions that they seem to think that taxing something will solve every problem.

Global warming? Tax carbon!

Smoking? Tax it!

Drinking? Tax it!

Taxing a problem is rarely the solution; it generates higher prices or drains valuable dollars from the economy.

However, when the local or regional government needs to get something done, and they don't have the tax dollars to effectively do so, they turn to the debt markets.

Unlike corporate debt, which receives interest that's taxed at your ordinary income tax rate. Municipal debt is exempt from federal taxes, and for many states that have state income tax, if you get municipal debt issued by your state, it will be exempt from state taxes as well. The reason that they need to do this is because there can be large projects like building a bridge, a new school, etc., that need to be funded immediately. The trickle of tax dollars slowly pouring in does not cover the immediate cost. Therefore, a municipal, state, or regional government agency will issue debt to attract investors, allowing them to fund their project and paying over time. The issue you can run into is that some of these projects don't generate revenue of their own, and this could create a large debt burden for local or state entities that can make it more difficult to function day to day, especially if they're already running large deficits. Some cities run big deficits because they assume they're collecting more tax than they're actually collecting. When they raise taxes, they drive investment out of the city. We've seen that large corporations have left Seattle due to higher taxes, leading to even larger deficits than estimated.

One goal of mine when looking at municipal debt is to find municipal debt that's actively revenue-generating on its own. This also means that when I look at a municipal debt fund, I want to see a self-generating focus as well. For example, I'd rather own municipal debt tied to a toll road than I would a municipal debt tied to local education.

Today, I want to look at a fund that focuses on this federal tax-free income and see how it can be a wonderful source of income for your portfolio.

Let's dive in!

Safety From Your Local Government

When the market is panicking, it's a good idea to hold some investments that have low credit risk. BNY Mellon Municipal Bond Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:DMB), yielding 3.7%, is a closed-end fund that invests in municipal bonds. Historically, municipal bonds have a significantly lower default rate than equally rated corporate bonds. We highlighted its benefits previously, and it has outperformed the market since.

We expect DMB to keep chugging along despite market volatility. The reason is that municipal bonds have very long terms. The average maturity for DMB's portfolio is slightly under 20 years. Source

The credit quality is high, with more than 50% of the portfolio having credit ratings of A or higher. Approximately 75% of the portfolio is "investment grade":

This makes DMB a great candidate to hold in a world where investors are fearful, and interest rates are declining.

The best part is that DMB is trading at about a 10% discount to NAV, allowing us to invest in this portfolio at a lower price than if we bought it directly.

The last time I discussed DMB, the most common objection was that the yield was lower than what we typically target – which is true; DMBs' distribution is relatively low. As NAV recovers, we expect that DMB will join peers who have already raised their dividends. As we're entering into a period where there could be turmoil in the markets and the risk of a recession is high, having a fund that's likely to start raising its distribution and have upside potential when everything else is falling is worth its weight in gold.

DMB has room to more than double its distribution just to match peers in the sector. DMB's restraint with the distribution is likely one reason why it's trading at a larger discount to NAV. It also means that as interest rates come down, municipal bond prices recover. DMB has retained more capital and will see a greater upside than its peers. Ultimately, this will allow it to pay out a higher distribution when management decides it's time to raise.

Investors who are patient and are willing to delay gratification will be rewarded. For less patient investors, Nuveen Quality Muni Income Fund (NAD) is an alternative option that's paying a higher distribution right now and has a very similar portfolio. Nuveen hiked the distribution of many of their muni-bond funds as a measure to reduce their discount to NAV. Is it sustainable? Only time will tell, but it does give the less patient investor a higher-yielding option.

Maybe you want to have a helping of both!

Either way, it's time to have municipals as part of your portfolio.

Conclusion

DMB is not an investment designed to outperform the market radically over the long term. An investment, it's designed to provide you with stable and steady federally tax-free income over the long run. Investing in the government's debt can be highly rewarding and a stable option. There's a reason why Treasury notes are viewed as exceptionally low risk – it's very rare that the government ever defaults on its debt. You can invest in individual municipal bonds on your own or use a fund like DMB and NAD to invest in a wide array of them, gaining exposure to many different great income-producing opportunities.
