In the end, I want your retirement to be flooded with income, saturating every aspect of your financial situation and providing you with a reservoir of excess income that you can then use to reinvest in your portfolio, save for hard days ahead, or generously give to others and to the causes you support. The greatest aspect of being an income investor is that the cash that arrives in your account is 100% yours to do whatever you want with. It's not in the hands of a management team who thinks they know better. It's not in the hands of a portfolio manager. It's in your hands. The best place for it to be.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Tariffied? 3 Of The Smartest Dividend Stocks To Buy Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

Last week was quite something, wasn't it?

As I have written in prior articles, it was the most stressful week in my career, as we were dealing with a myriad of political and macroeconomic news that created market-moving headlines on a daily basis (literally).

Using the handy overview below, we see, once again, what we went through, as not a single day was anywhere close to 'normal.' On Monday, the Cboe Volatility Index made it to 60, on Tuesday, the S&P 500 fell 1.6% on massive volumes, followed by a 9.5% rally on Wednesday.

Then, on Thursday, the market fell by 3.5%, followed by a jump of almost 2% on Friday. Also, during this week, the dollar lost nearly 3%.

To make matters worse, the numbers above do not show the intraday swings that traders and investors had to deal with. Looking at closing prices is one thing. The drama that comes between 9:30 AM and 4:00 PM is another thing.

When including low-to-high swings, the market has the highest volatility since the Great Financial Crisis, as even the 2020 pandemic had more predictable headlines. I don't want to sound repetitive, but that's quite something.

Even after President Trump put a pause on new tariffs except for China, the market remained nervous, as the biggest risk became confidence in the United States and the elevated risks of a recession tied to the trade war with China.

I believe the quote below hits the nail on the head in light of rising longer-term bond yields despite a weaker dollar and lower inflation:

“Confidence outside the US on the quality and leadership of US assets across equity, fixed income and currency has been damaged,” said Nathan Thooft at Manulife Investment Management in Boston, which oversees $160 billion. “The question remains is this a temporary hit or a secular shift? We still believe the former. But that doesn’t dismiss the fact there are large asset owners who are looking for substitutes and diversification for safe haven assets.” - Bloomberg

In light of this, it's quite interesting to note that while the S&P 500 is down 9% year-to-date, the EURO STOXX 50 ETF (FEZ) is up 10%. Roughly half of these gains are coming from a strong euro. But still...

Now, the question on everyone's mind is the same.

Is This A Buyable Bottom?

I don't know. And neither does anyone else.

Even Wall Street's smartest people cannot tell you if this is a bottom.

Anyone who pretends otherwise is lying.

But that's OK.

What we can do is assess the risk/reward, which is something we always do, as it's our best weapon against uncertainty.

Goldman Sachs, for example, uses learnings from prior bear markets to assess what it takes for stocks to bottom.

For starters, there is a huge difference between cyclical and event-driven bear markets. The first one is very serious, while the other is often nasty and short. Essentially, it explains why everyone is so nervous, as avoiding a steep recession is critical to keep this an event-driven bear market.

The average cyclical and event-driven bear markets generally tend to fall around 30%, although they differ in terms of duration. Cyclical bear markets last an average of around two years and take about five years to fully rebound to their starting point, while the event-driven ones tend to last around eight months and recover in about a year. - Goldman Sachs

The good news is that most bear markets recover within a year. However, they often require triggers. Goldman noted four of them:

  • Stocks need to be attractively valued.
  • Investor sentiment/positioning needs to be very pessimistic.
  • The stock market/economy needs policy support.
  • We need second-derivative tailwinds. This means the rate of change (growth rates) needs to improve. For example, economic contraction becomes less severe.

Let's look into this.

For now, I believe we're in an event-driven scenario, as President Trump's tariffs triggered the global sell-off. Right off the bat, that's good to know.

With regard to valuations, we're basically still in a tricky scenario, as we need to be selective.

The S&P 500 trades at 19x earnings, which is not very cheap compared to historical averages. However, the equal-weight S&P 500 is far from overvalued at 15x earnings, as the market-weighted S&P 500 is still very top-heavy.

Meanwhile, mid-cap stocks are very cheap.

In other words, we are not necessarily at levels that would suggest the end of a correction, especially because tariffs could pressure earnings, which is often an overlooked factor.

However, many value stocks are certainly cheap.

So, what about investor sentiment?

Investors are bearish. As of April 9, 59% of retail investors expected lower stock prices over the next six months, almost twice as many as the historic average of the AAII survey. That's the kind of sentiment you want to see when buying stocks.

We are seeing similar developments in the Fear & Greed index, which is at 13, a number indicative of 'Extreme Fear.' While it's off the lows, it is still below its 2024 lows and driven by poor market breadth, a lot of put buying, and elevated volatility.

So, what about policy support?

This is where it gets tricky.

  • Although inflation has come down, we are unlikely to get monetary support, as the Fed will have to wait and see what the impact of tariffs will be on inflation (and growth). Right now, the market expects one rate cut in June, another one in July, and another one in September. However, conviction isn't high, as we can see below. Personally, I do not believe we will get a cut unless fiscal policy risks have faded.

  • The biggest risk is policy headwinds, as new tariffs could increase uncertainty, boost recession odds, and cause other issues. However, I believe policy risks could be bullish. For starters, China tariffs have reached such a high level that we are basically looking at a trade embargo between China and the U.S. Even if tariffs were to go to 1,000% (just as an example), not a lot would change. Moreover, because China is much more dependent on the U.S. than the other way around, I believe China will be forced into talks that could cause some very positive headlines. In general, I believe the market's reaction to bond turmoil will make it more likely that tariff risks fall, as the U.S. is not in a good spot to deal with elevated long-term bonds.

Last but not least, growth rates matter.

However, right now, that's not the most important indicator, as weird as that may sound. After all, we're still in an event-driven correction. It all depends on policies and their impact on growth.

Nobody really knows what inflation will look like next month or how badly earnings will be hit by tariffs.

For now, its real GDP is expected to contract. However, since early April, growth expectations have rebounded a bit. If this continues, this factor could be bullish as well. But then again, it's a bit early to make that call.

So, where do we stand?

For now, we're dealing with an event-driven correction, triggered by tariffs and amplified by uncertainty. While some value stocks are attractively priced, especially in the mid-cap space, the broader market isn't super cheap.

Sentiment, on the other hand, is deeply bearish, which is typically a positive contrarian signal.

Meanwhile, policy support remains uncertain, as the Fed is boxed in by inflation risks and political noise.

Still, I believe policy risks could ultimately work in the market's favor.

Growth data is mixed, and while it's not the main story right now, any rebound there could add fuel to a recovery.

With that said, going into this week, I want to share three dividend ideas with you that, I believe, are great buys during this uncertainty, as they all bring something to the table that could make them even stronger players in the years ahead than before the tariff turmoil started(!).

Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) - One Of My Favorite Dividend Growers With Re-Shoring Benefits

Last week, I expanded my investment in Old Dominion Freight Line by 65%, making it my fourth-largest position, accounting for 5.2% of my portfolio.

This was based on a number of reasons, including its poor performance, as it's a cyclical less-than-truckload ("LTL") carrier. Whenever recession fears start to rise, investors dump these stocks.

With that said, I dislike the trucking industry, in general, as it's capital-intensive, very competitive, and prone to disruptions like labor shortages.

The good news is that LTL is different, as companies make shorter trips and can use their distribution network to build a competitive edge. That's why ODFL has a "narrow moat" tag from Morningstar, which is very unique in its industry.

This narrow moat is based on 261 service/distribution centers in 48 states. The majority of these centers are owned by the company, which gives it a major real estate footprint. This network makes it the second-largest LTL provider behind FedEx Freight, which is set to become an independent company through a spin-off.

ODFL is so efficient that it has grown its revenue by 10.3% per year since 2004. This is entirely organic, meaning no major M&A has boosted these results.

Moreover, it is, by far, the best LTL provider, as it has a claims ratio of a mere 0.1% and an on-time performance of 99%. It has also won the Mastio Quality Award 15 years in a row.

This does not just sound good, but also indicates pricing power.

Using the Mastio numbers below, we see that ODFL has a superior service level, which comes with pricing power, as most peers have worse service levels and only barely lower prices.

This comes with inflation protection, as ODFL used to charge close to $300 per shipment in 2015. Now, that number is close to $500.

Moreover, it has an operating ratio of roughly 73%, which is super efficient, as most trucking companies have operating ratios close to 100%. The operating ratio indicates how much it costs to run the business, as a percentage of total revenues.

Railroads usually have operating ratios in the 60% range. A 75% number is truly phenomenal.

Even better, the company has negative net debt, meaning it has more cash than gross debt. This, too, adds a layer of stability and allows the company to invest in growth without having to tap the debt market.

The only bad news is that ODFL yields just 0.7%.

Fortunately, this dividend has a payout ratio of a mere 19% and a five-year CAGR of 34%.

Moreover, despite losing more than a third of its value, the company is still outperforming the market, as its ten-year total return is a staggering 555%, a mile above the market's impressive 202% return.

Going forward, I believe ODFL will continue to beat the market, as it could be one of the biggest winners of economic re-shoring. Even if President Trump were to eliminate most tariffs, re-shoring will likely accelerate, as companies de-risk their supply chains somewhat.

As LTL companies have an industrial focus, they will benefit tremendously. ODFL, especially, will be in a good spot, as it can use its balance sheet to expand more efficiently, while its service quality will help it to win new business deals.

With regard to its valuation, the company now trades at its 10-year average P/E ratio of 27.9x. While it needs to be seen how bad the impact of tariffs will be, analysts expect 18% and 17% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027.

If we get a more sustainable uptrend, I expect ODFL to be a major winner and enjoy a lot more business wins in the years ahead.

While making it my #4 position made my portfolio more volatile, it's a 'bet' I feel extremely good about.

ONEOK (OKE) - 'Mission-Critical' Income

I thought about this pick quite a bit, as I have discussed ONEOK a lot this year.

However, I think it deserves even more attention, as I think investors who sold the stock made a big mistake.

While one may make the case that ONEOK was a bit overvalued when it ran to almost $120 per share in November, the 30% sell-off since then seems to be overblown.

This brings me to the reason why OKE is included in this article.

Because of the trade war with China, the market feared it could lead to less Chinese demand for natural gas and natural gas liquids, which are two commodities (commodity groups, to be precise) that are needed by the Chinese.

For example, propane, which is a natural gas liquid, has seen massive export growth, reaching 2 million barrels per day at the end of 2024.

Generally speaking, the country requires a lot of natural gas liquids, which also include butane, isobutane, pentane, and ethane. These are used in a wide range of operations, including industrial production, residential heating, and transportation.

In light of the trade war, demand is expected to fall due to rising recession risks and trade embargoes.

However, China does not have alternatives, as Reuters reported:

Russia's exporters of liquefied petroleum gas are unlikely to be able to provide enough LPG to make up for U.S. supplies disrupted by tariffs, traders said on Thursday, citing a lack of infrastructure.

Industry insiders said that Chinese petrochemical makers that buy U.S. LPG worth $11 billion annually are poised to cut output or shut for maintenance in the coming weeks as retaliatory tariffs on U.S. imports drive up costs. - Reuters

While recessions will temporarily hurt demand, I see no damage to America's success in natural gas liquids. In fact, I am super bullish on it, as there's no country on earth in a better spot to service the world's rising demand for these commodities than the U.S. For example, the Permian Basin alone produces more than half of OPEC's entire natural gas liquids volume.

That's where ONEOK comes in, as it owns a 60,000-mile pipeline network that transports natural gas liquids, refined products, crude oil, and natural gas.

Close to 40% of its revenue this year is expected to come from natural gas liquids, followed by gathering/processing activities (28%), and refined/crude oil shipments (27%).

The best thing about this network is that it makes ONEOK 'mission-critical' in servicing domestic and export demand. It also benefits from mostly take-or-pay contracts, which protect the company even if volumes were to decline.

That is very important, as current oil and gas prices are very subdued.

Even during the 2014/2025 oil price crash and the 2020/2021 pandemic, the company did not cut its dividend and grew its EBITDA. This year, EBITDA is expected to grow by 21%, potentially followed by 10% growth in 2026.

It is also deploying at least $2.3 billion in growth CapEx this year to exploit strong secular growth:

Key projects have included NGL pipeline expansions in the Bakken and Permian Basins, additional NGL fractionation capacity in Mont Belvieu and the Mid-Continent, refined products pipeline expansions into the Denver market and natural gas storage expansions in Oklahoma and Texas. Most recently, we announced an LPG export project joint venture with MPLX. With LPG exports added to our service mix, we will provide an integrated NGL wellhead-to-water solution for our customers enhancing our product offerings. - OKE 4Q24 Earnings Call

With regard to its dividend, currently yielding 5.0%, the dividend is expected to grow by 3-4% per year going forward. I do not expect any major recession to derail these plans.

The next one is a bit more cyclical.

United Rentals (URI) - Equipment To Make America Great Again

Growth expectations have taken a hit in the U.S., as we discussed in the first part of this article. This has hit cyclical stocks like ODFL.

Another stock that has been hit is United Rentals, a fast-growing company focused on equipment rentals. Founded in 1997, it has used high organic growth and aggressive M&A to capture a market share of 15%, with a large footprint in specialty equipment.

Because the company has such a large footprint and equipment for a wide range of tasks, including infrastructure, residential, pipelines, chemicals, industrial manufacturing, power generation, and more, it benefits from higher growth expectations.

As a result of economic expectations coming down recently, URI is trading 35% below its 52-week high, one of its biggest drawdowns in years. Despite this, it has returned more than 520% over the past ten years.

Moreover, the company plans another >50 new locations in 2025, which underscores its methodical expansion into underserved markets.

These greenfield sites often focus on specialty products like matting (acquired via the 2022 BlueLine deal) or climate solutions, which come with high margins and benefit from secular trends like infrastructure spending and workplace safety regulations.

With regard to shareholder distributions, the company enjoys a super healthy balance sheet with a 1.8x leverage ratio, $2.8 billion in liquidity, and no major maturities until 2027.

Because United Rentals has matured in recent years, it started a dividend program in 1Q23. It recently hiked this dividend by 10%. It currently yields 1.2% with a payout ratio of a mere 15%.

In other words, there's a lot of dividend growth potential, which comes with aggressive buybacks, as URI has bought back 9% of its shares over the past three years alone.

Although the current environment is tough, I believe URI is a critical part of Trump's plan to revive American manufacturing and broaden economic growth.

Especially in light of data center growth, pipeline shortages, factory construction, and other large projects, URI has a front seat in America's economy.

Trading at 13.4x earnings, the company is expected to grow its EPS by 11-12% in 2026 and 2027.

While these numbers are subject to change (now more than ever), I am very bullish on URI and would be a buyer if I did not already own Caterpillar (CAT), which is a correlated play.

Takeaway

Last week was one for the history books, as we saw brutal volatility, policy chaos, and a lot of fear.

I can't say with certainty whether we've seen the bottom, but I can say this: the setup increasingly looks like an event-driven correction, not a prolonged downturn.

Valuations in some corners of the market, especially mid-caps and value stocks, are attractive, sentiment is bearish (which is good), and policy risks might actually become tailwinds.

That's why I'm using this environment to buy quality stocks that bring something special to the table.

In this article, we discussed three of them. One with a high yield and two with low yields but aggressive growth.

Going forward, I expect all of these stocks to beat the market and deliver consistent dividend growth.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is prolonged policy uncertainty.

If tariff tensions escalate further or if the Federal Reserve has to delay rate cuts due to sticky inflation, market volatility could remain elevated and pressure the stock market.
